questions.question: What naming convention must all custom hooks follow?
options: Start with 'get'
Start with 'use'
Start with 'hook'
Start with 'handle'
questions.explanation: React identifies hooks by the 'use' prefix. This naming convention also enables linting rules (eslint-plugin-react-hooks) to enforce the Rules of Hooks.

questions.question: Why should a useFetch hook return a cleanup function from useEffect?
options: To reset state when the URL changes
To abort the in-flight fetch request when the component unmounts or URL changes
To save the response to localStorage
To prevent the useEffect from running twice in development
questions.answer: To abort the in-flight fetch request when the component unmounts or URL changes
questions.explanation: Without cleanup, an unmounted component might try to call setState after unmounting, causing memory leaks and React warnings. AbortController.abort() cancels the fetch.

questions.question: What does a useDebounce hook prevent?
options: Prevents state updates during renders
Prevents excessive API calls by delaying the update until the user stops typing
Prevents React from batching state updates
Prevents memory leaks in event handlers
questions.answer: Prevents excessive API calls by delaying the update until the user stops typing
questions.explanation: useDebounce delays the propagation of a value until it stops changing for a specified delay, reducing the frequency of expensive operations like API calls during search input.

questions.question: Do two components using the same custom hook share state?
options: Yes, custom hooks act as singletons
No, each component call creates completely isolated state
Yes, if they're in the same parent component
Only if the hook uses useContext
questions.answer: No, each component call creates completely isolated state
questions.explanation: Custom hooks are just functions. Each call to a hook creates a fresh, isolated instance of all the state and effects inside it — just like calling useState() in multiple components.

questions.question: What should a useLocalStorage hook use to read the initial value lazily?
options: useEffect with an empty dependency array
A useState initializer function (lazy initialization)
useLayoutEffect
useRef to avoid re-renders
questions.explanation: useState(() => localStorage.getItem(key)) uses lazy initialization — the function only runs once on mount, avoiding repeated localStorage reads on every render.
